title: Apartments for Rent in Cleveland Under $1,000
metaTitle: Apartments for Rent in Cleveland Under $1,000
metaDescription: Looking for apartments for rent in Cleveland under $1,000? See real rent ranges by size, where to look, and voucher-friendly options. Book a free showing.
answerBox: Yes — apartments under $1,000 a month are common in Cleveland, where citywide rents run well below the national average. Studios and smaller one-bedrooms often land near or under that mark, and two-bedroom homes in our own selection has recently averaged under $1,000, including Section 8-friendly options across the city's east and southeast side neighborhoods.

sections.h2: Are there apartments for rent in Cleveland under $1,000?
body: Yes. Cleveland's overall median asking rent was **$1,250/month** as of Zumper's July 4, 2026 report, but that citywide figure blends everything from downtown lofts to small neighborhood buildings — plenty of individual listings, especially studios, one-bedrooms, and older-building units, price under $1,000. Apartments.com listed more than **1,000 active Cleveland-area apartment listings under $1,000/month** as of a check in July 2026, so the under-$1,000 segment of the market is large, not a narrow exception.

Our own currently available Cleveland apartments back this up: the two-bedroom units we've had available recently have ranged from about **$750 to $1,100 a month, averaging under $1,000**, as of July 2026 (availability changes constantly, so [book a free showing](/p/book-showing) to see what's open now). Every home we work with also accepts Housing Choice Vouchers, which can make a unit at this price point even more affordable for qualifying households.

sections.h2: How much do Cleveland apartments cost by size?
body: Two different rent-tracking methodologies give two legitimate but different pictures of the Cleveland market. Zumper's July 2026 report reflects the broader median across all currently listed apartments, including smaller and older buildings, while RentCafe (Yardi)'s July 2026 report averages rents specifically at larger, professionally managed apartment complexes — which tend to run higher. Neither number is "the" Cleveland rent; together they show the real range a renter searching under $1,000 will encounter.

sections.h2: Where in Cleveland to look for lower-cost apartments
body: The lowest-cost apartment inventory tends to concentrate in Cleveland's older residential neighborhoods rather than downtown or University Circle. Our own Section 8-ready rental homes are concentrated on the city's **east and southeast side**, in neighborhoods including Slavic Village (ZIP 44105), Collinwood (44110), Glenville (44108), Fairfax and Central (44104), Hough (44103), and the Buckeye-Shaker area (44120), plus a smaller number of homes on the west side around Cudell/Detroit-Shoreway (44102) and Old Brooklyn (44109).

Housing stock in these neighborhoods is mostly early-1900s duplexes, triplexes, and fourplexes rather than large apartment complexes, which is part of why per-unit rents can run lower than the citywide apartment averages above. See our [Slavic Village rental guide](/cleveland-rentals/neighborhoods/houses-for-rent-slavic-village-cleveland/) for one specific example of pricing and transit in a lower-cost east-side ZIP.

sections.h2: Do apartments under $1,000 accept Section 8 vouchers in Cleveland?
body: Every home Rent Finder Cleveland works with accepts Housing Choice Vouchers (Section 8) and is HUD-inspection-ready — that includes our lower-priced units, not just a subset. Vouchers are administered countywide through the **Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority (CMHA)**, and a voucher covering part of the rent can make a unit that's already under $1,000 even more affordable for a qualifying household. Read our full [Section 8 housing guide](/section-8-housing-cleveland-oh/) for how applications, waitlists, and inspections work.

sections.h2: How to tour and apply for an affordable Cleveland apartment
body: The process is the same regardless of price point: [book a free showing](/p/book-showing) to tour a currently available unit, then apply through our online [application portal](/apply) if it's a fit. We review income, rental history, and any voucher or co-signer support as part of one consistent screening process — we don't promise to waive that review, but affordability at this price band, plus voucher eligibility, both help. If you're comparing under-$1,000 apartments to a slightly higher budget, see our guide to [apartments under $1,200](/cleveland-rentals/apartments/apartments-for-rent-cleveland-under-1200/) or browse [cheap apartments across Cleveland](/cleveland-rentals/apartments/cheap-apartments-for-rent-cleveland/) more broadly.

faq.q: Can I really find an apartment in Cleveland for under $1,000 a month?
faq.a: Yes. Cleveland's median asking rent citywide was $1,250 as of Zumper's July 2026 report, but studios, one-bedrooms, and many two-bedroom units in older neighborhood buildings commonly price under $1,000. Apartments.com listed over 1,000 active Cleveland-area listings under that mark as of July 2026.

faq.q: What size apartment can I get for under $1,000 in Cleveland?
faq.a: Studios, one-bedrooms, and many two-bedroom units fall under or near $1,000, depending on the building and neighborhood. Our own currently available two-bedroom units have recently averaged under $1,000, ranging from about $750 to $1,100 a month as of July 2026.

faq.q: Where are the most affordable apartments in Cleveland?
faq.a: Lower-cost inventory tends to concentrate in east and southeast side neighborhoods such as Slavic Village (44105), Collinwood (44110), Glenville (44108), and Fairfax/Central (44104), where housing stock is mostly older duplexes and small multi-family buildings rather than large complexes.

faq.q: Do cheap Cleveland apartments accept Section 8 vouchers?
faq.a: Every home Rent Finder Cleveland works with, at every price point, accepts Housing Choice Vouchers and is HUD-inspection-ready. Vouchers are administered countywide through CMHA and can make an already-affordable unit even more accessible for qualifying households.

faq.q: Will I still need to pass a screening for a low-cost apartment?
faq.a: Yes. We apply the same income, rental history, and reference review to every applicant regardless of rent price — a lower price point doesn't come with a lighter application review. A voucher, co-signer, or strong references can all help strengthen an application.
